problems.hint: Redraw exactly when the first number is below what a fresh draw is worth.
problems.solution:
A fresh draw is worth $\tfrac12$, so keep the first draw iff it exceeds $\tfrac12$:
$$E = \underbrace{P(X \ge \tfrac12)\,E[X \mid X \ge \tfrac12]}_{\frac12\cdot\frac34} + \underbrace{P(X < \tfrac12)\cdot\frac12}_{\frac12\cdot\frac12} = \frac38 + \frac14 = \frac58 = 0.625.$$
With $n$ total draws allowed the value satisfies $V_{n} = \frac{1 + V_{n-1}^2}{2}$ — worth remembering for the multi-redraw follow-up.

problems.title: Rolling Until It's Matched
problems.question: Francisco rolls a fair die and records the value. He then keeps rolling until he obtains a value at least as large as his first roll. Let $N$ be the number of rolls *after* the first. Find $E[N]$.
problems.hint: Given the first roll is $v$, the wait is geometric. Average $\frac{6}{7-v}$ over $v$ — a harmonic number appears.
problems.solution:
Given first roll $v$, each later roll succeeds ($\ge v$) with probability $\frac{7-v}{6}$, so the conditional wait is $\frac{6}{7-v}$. Averaging:
$$E[N] = \frac16\sum_{v=1}^{6}\frac{6}{7-v} = \sum_{k=1}^{6}\frac{1}{k} = H_6 = \frac{49}{20} = 2.45.$$
The harmonic number shows up because high first rolls are rare but very slow to match — heavy conditional waits balanced by small weights.

problems.title: The Middle Die
problems.question: Mike rolls three fair 6-sided dice at once. What is the expected value of the median of the three?
problems.hint: What does the symmetry $x \mapsto 7 - x$ do to the median?
problems.solution:
The map $x \mapsto 7 - x$ preserves the joint distribution of the dice and sends the median $M$ to $7 - M$. So $M$ and $7 - M$ have the same distribution:
$$E[M] = E[7 - M] \;\Rightarrow\; E[M] = \frac72 = 3.5.$$
Same as a single die — symmetry beats the order-statistics integral. (The *max* is where the interesting number lives: $E[\max] = \frac{119}{24}$.)

problems.title: Minimum-Variance Blend
problems.question: Let $X_1$ and $X_2$ be independent with variances 25 and 100. Find the $c \in [0,1]$ minimizing the variance of $Y = cX_1 + (1-c)X_2$.
problems.hint: Differentiate $c^2\cdot 25 + (1-c)^2\cdot 100$ — or recall inverse-variance weighting.
problems.solution:
$\mathrm{Var}(Y) = 25c^2 + 100(1-c)^2$; setting the derivative to zero: $50c = 200(1-c)$, so
$$c = \frac{100}{125} = 0.8.$$
General rule: optimal weights are proportional to *inverse* variances ($c \propto 1/25$, $1-c \propto 1/100$) — the same math behind minimum-variance portfolios and precision-weighted averaging.

problems.title: A Random Antisymmetric Determinant
problems.question: Let $A$ be a random $2\times 2$ matrix with each entry independently $\mathrm{Bernoulli}(0.5)$. Find $E[\det(A - A^T)]$.
problems.hint: Write out $A - A^T$ — it has a very special form.
problems.solution:
$A - A^T = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & d \\ -d & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ with $d = A_{12} - A_{21}$, so $\det = d^2$.

$d$ takes values $-1, 0, 1$ with probabilities $\tfrac14, \tfrac12, \tfrac14$:
$$E[d^2] = \frac14 + \frac14 = \frac12.$$
The diagonal entries never mattered — they cancel in $A - A^T$.

problems.title: One Re-Flip Each
problems.question: You flip 8 fair coins at once, then may re-flip any subset of them, each coin at most once. You receive $\$1$ per head at the end. Playing optimally, what is the fair value of the game?
problems.hint: Decide coin by coin — what's the value of one coin that you may re-flip once?
problems.solution:
Coins are independent, so optimize each alone: keep heads, re-flip tails. One coin is a head at the end unless it shows tails twice:
$$P(\text{head}) = 1 - \frac14 = \frac34, \qquad E = 8\cdot\frac34 = \$6.$$

problems.title: Turning the Basket Blue
problems.question: A basket holds 2 red and 1 blue orbs. Each turn you draw an orb uniformly at random and replace it with a blue orb (whatever its color). Find the expected number of draws until the basket contains only blue orbs.
problems.hint: Track the number of red orbs; each stage is a geometric wait.
problems.solution:
With $r$ reds among 3 orbs, each draw removes a red with probability $\frac{r}{3}$ — a geometric wait of $\frac{3}{r}$ draws. From $r = 2$:
$$E = \frac{3}{2} + \frac{3}{1} = 4.5.$$

problems.title: The Winner's Curse
problems.question: You bid on a painting whose value $V$ is uniform on $(\$0, \$100{,}000)$. If your bid exceeds $V$, you win it (paying your bid) and sell it to a museum for $1.5V$. What bid maximizes your profit? (If no bid can profit, bid $\$0$.)
problems.hint: If your bid $b$ wins, what do you learn about $V$ — and what is the painting worth to you *conditional on winning*?
problems.solution:
Winning is bad news: conditional on $V < b$, the value averages only $\frac{b}{2}$, so your resale nets $1.5\cdot\frac{b}{2} = 0.75b$ — less than the $b$ you paid:
$$E[\text{profit}] = \frac{b}{100000}\left(0.75b - b\right) = -\frac{0.25\,b^2}{100000} < 0 \text{ for all } b > 0.$$
Optimal bid: $\$0$. This is the winner's curse — the 1.5× markup isn't enough to overcome the adverse selection of only winning overpriced items. (You'd need a multiplier above 2 to bid at all.)

problems.title: Two Sixes in a Row
problems.question: On average, how many rolls of a fair 6-sided die are needed to observe two 6s in a row?
problems.hint: Streak formula: $E = p^{-1} + p^{-2}$ for a run of two with success probability $p$.
problems.solution:
With $E_1$ = expected rolls to a first 6 and the streak recursion $E_2 = \frac{E_1 + 1}{p}$:
$$E = \frac{1}{1/6} + \frac{1}{(1/6)^2} = 6 + 36 = 42.$$
Companion to the coin's HH $= 2 + 4 = 6$ — same structure, rarer success.

problems.title: Push Your Luck on a d10
problems.question: Adam rolls a fair 10-sided die repeatedly. Each non-10 roll adds its face value to his bank; rolling a 10 wipes the bank to zero and ends nothing — he may stop at any time and keep the bank. Playing optimally, Adam should stop once his bank reaches at least $\$k$. Find $k$.
problems.hint: Compare the marginal EV of one more roll at bank $t$: what do you gain on average, and what do you risk?
problems.solution:
At bank $t$, one more roll gains the average non-10 face $\frac{1+\cdots+9}{10} = 4.5$ but loses $t$ with probability $\frac{1}{10}$:
$$\Delta EV = 4.5 - \frac{t}{10},$$
which is positive exactly while $t < 45$. So Adam keeps rolling below $45$ and stops at
$$k = 45.$$
(The myopic one-step rule is optimal here because the threshold structure makes future decisions consistent with it.)

problems.title: The Wait That Never Averages
problems.question: Let $U \sim \mathrm{Unif}(0,1)$ and $X_1, X_2, \dots \sim \mathrm{Unif}(0,1)$ IID, independent of $U$. Define $N = \min\{n : X_n > U\}$. Find $E[N]$. Enter $-1$ if the answer is infinite.
problems.hint: $E[N \mid U]$ is a geometric mean — then integrate over $U$ and watch the endpoint.
problems.solution:
Given $U = u$, $N$ is Geometric($1-u$) with $E[N \mid U = u] = \frac{1}{1-u}$. Averaging:
$$E[N] = \int_0^1 \frac{du}{1-u} = \infty.$$
Every conditional expectation is finite, yet the overall expectation diverges — the rare draws of $U$ near 1 contribute unboundedly. ($N$ is finite almost surely; its *mean* isn't. Interviewers use this to test whether you check integrability rather than assume it.)

problems.title: All Six Faces
problems.question: On average, how many rolls of a fair 6-sided die does it take to observe all six faces at least once?
problems.hint: Coupon collector: sum the geometric waits as the number of unseen faces shrinks.
problems.solution:
When $k$ faces remain unseen, the wait for a new one is Geometric($\frac{k}{6}$), taking $\frac{6}{k}$ rolls on average:
$$E = 6\left(\frac16 + \frac15 + \frac14 + \frac13 + \frac12 + 1\right) = 6H_6 = \frac{147}{10} = 14.7.$$
The canonical coupon-collector number — worth having memorized.

problems.title: Coin on the Chessboard
problems.question: A chessboard is made of 2-inch $\times$ 2-inch squares. A coin of diameter 1 inch is tossed and lands uniformly at random on the board. What is the probability that the coin lies completely within a single square (touching no gridline)?
problems.hint: Track the coin's center. How far must it stay from every gridline?
problems.solution:
The coin avoids all gridlines exactly when its center is at least $\frac12$ inch from every edge of the square it lands in. Within a $2 \times 2$ square, the allowed region for the center is the inner $1 \times 1$ square, so
$$P = \frac{1 \cdot 1}{2 \cdot 2} = \frac14.$$
A staple geometric-probability screen: always reduce a tossed disk to its center point.

problems.title: One Fair Coin Saves Parity
problems.question: $n$ coins are laid out in front of you. One is fair; the other $n-1$ each show heads with probability $\lambda$, where $0 < \lambda < 1$. All $n$ coins are flipped. Find the probability that an even number of heads appears.
problems.hint: Condition on the biased coins first. What does the single fair coin do to the parity?
problems.solution:
Condition on the total showing on the $n-1$ biased coins. Whatever their parity, the fair coin makes the overall parity even with probability exactly $\frac12$: if the biased total is even we need tails ($\frac12$), if odd we need heads ($\frac12$). So
$$P[\text{even}] = \frac12,$$
independent of $n$ and $\lambda$. One genuinely fair coin completely randomizes parity — a trick worth remembering for any XOR-flavored question.

problems.title: Rigging the Candy Boxes
problems.question: You have 5 good candies, 5 bad candies, and two boxes. You may distribute all 10 candies between the boxes however you like. Your blindfolded brother then picks a box uniformly at random and draws one candy uniformly at random from it, which you receive. Playing optimally, what is the probability you receive a good candy?
problems.hint: Make one box as pure as possible while keeping the other box as good as possible.
problems.solution:
Put a single good candy alone in box 1 and the remaining 4 good + 5 bad in box 2. Then
$$P[\text{good}] = \frac12 \cdot 1 + \frac12 \cdot \frac49 = \frac{13}{18} \approx 0.7222.$$
A brute-force check over all $(g, b)$ splits confirms this is optimal. The intuition: a guaranteed win from one box costs only one good candy, and the other box stays nearly balanced.

problems.title: Rain Without Independence
problems.question: The chance of rain is 40% on Saturday and 70% on Sunday. Without assuming independence, find the tightest possible upper bound on the probability that it does not rain at all this weekend.
problems.hint: Bound $P[A \cap B]$ by the smaller of $P[A]$ and $P[B]$ — then check the bound is achievable.
problems.solution:
Let $A$ = no rain Saturday ($P[A] = 0.6$) and $B$ = no rain Sunday ($P[B] = 0.3$). For any joint distribution,
$$P[A \cap B] \le \min(P[A], P[B]) = 0.3,$$
and the bound is attained when $B \subseteq A$ (whenever Sunday is dry, Saturday was too). So the tightest upper limit is $0.3$. This is the upper Fréchet bound — the standard follow-up to any "assume independence" warm-up.

problems.title: Air and Water Before Fire
problems.question: Four cards labelled water, earth, fire, and air lie face down. You flip them over one at a time in uniformly random order. You win if both the air and water cards are revealed before the fire card. What is the probability that you win?
problems.hint: The earth card is irrelevant. Where must fire sit among the three cards that matter?
problems.solution:
Only the relative order of water, air, and fire matters, and all $3! = 6$ orderings are equally likely. You win exactly when fire is last of the three, which happens in $2! = 2$ orderings:
$$P = \frac{2}{6} = \frac13.$$
Equivalently: fire is equally likely to be first, middle, or last among the three relevant cards.

problems.title: Five Before Snake Eyes
problems.question: Two fair dice are rolled repeatedly. What is the probability that a sum of 5 appears before a sum of 2?
problems.hint: Ignore every roll that is neither. What is the conditional probability that a decisive roll is a 5?
problems.solution:
Rolls that show neither sum can be ignored. Given a decisive roll, the probability it is a 5 is
$$P = \frac{P[\text{sum } 5]}{P[\text{sum } 5] + P[\text{sum } 2]} = \frac{4/36}{4/36 + 1/36} = \frac45.$$
The race-between-events reduction — the engine behind every craps-style question.

problems.title: A Random Subset of a Random Subset
problems.question: Subsets $A$ and $B$ are chosen independently and uniformly at random from all subsets of a set $X$ with $|X| = 5$. What is the probability that $A \subseteq B$?
problems.hint: Decide element by element. For each element, which of the four (in $A$?, in $B$?) combinations are allowed?
problems.solution:
Each element of $X$ independently lands in one of four equally likely cells: in both, in $A$ only, in $B$ only, or in neither. The containment $A \subseteq B$ fails only for "in $A$ only", so each element complies with probability $\frac34$:
$$P = \left(\frac34\right)^5 = \frac{243}{1024} \approx 0.2373.$$
Product-over-elements is the standard way to tame random-subset questions.

problems.title: The Twice-Red Painted Cube
problems.question: A white $3 \times 3 \times 3$ cube is painted red on its outer surface, then cut into 27 unit cubes. One unit cube is selected uniformly at random and rolled twice; both rolls show a red face. What is the probability the selected cube is a corner cube?
problems.hint: Corner, edge, face, and center cubes have 3, 2, 1, 0 red faces. Apply Bayes with squared likelihoods.
problems.solution:
The 27 cubes split into 8 corners (3 red faces), 12 edges (2), 6 face-centers (1), and 1 center (0). Rolling red twice has likelihood $(r/6)^2$ for a cube with $r$ red faces. By Bayes,
$$P[\text{corner} \mid RR] = \frac{8 \cdot (1/2)^2}{8 \cdot (1/2)^2 + 12 \cdot (1/3)^2 + 6 \cdot (1/6)^2} = \frac{2}{2 + 4/3 + 1/6} = \frac47 \approx 0.5714.$$
The repeated observation squares each likelihood — the same structure as the double-headed-coin problem.

problems.title: Positive Given a Positive Sum
problems.question: Let $X, Y \sim N(0,1)$ be IID. Find $P[X > 0 \mid X + Y > 0]$.
problems.hint: Draw the plane. Both events are half-planes through the origin — the answer is a ratio of angles.
problems.solution:
The vector $(X, Y)$ is rotationally symmetric about the origin, so the probability of any cone is proportional to its angle. The event $\{x + y > 0\}$ is the half-plane above the line $y = -x$, spanning the directions from $-45^\circ$ to $135^\circ$. Its overlap with $\{x > 0\}$ (directions $-90^\circ$ to $90^\circ$) spans $-45^\circ$ to $90^\circ$, i.e. $135^\circ$ of the $180^\circ$ half-plane:
$$P = \frac{135^\circ}{180^\circ} = \frac34.$$
Rotational symmetry turns bivariate normal questions into geometry.

problems.title: Three Chords, No Crossings
problems.question: Six points are chosen uniformly at random on a circle, then split uniformly at random into three pairs, and a chord is drawn through each pair. What is the probability that no two chords intersect?
problems.hint: Count the ways to pair 6 points on a circle, and count the non-crossing pairings (a Catalan number).
problems.solution:
Only the pairing matters, not the point positions. Six labelled points admit $5!! = 5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 = 15$ perfect matchings, all equally likely. The non-crossing matchings of $2n$ points on a circle number the Catalan number $C_n$; for $n = 3$, $C_3 = 5$. So
$$P = \frac{5}{15} = \frac13.$$
Worth memorizing: random chords non-crossing $= C_n / (2n-1)!!$.

problems.title: The German Tank Problem
problems.question: German tanks carry distinct serial numbers $1, 2, \dots, N$. You observe 6 tanks with serial numbers 38, 17, 59, 42, 97, and 120. Under the frequentist (minimum-variance unbiased) approach, what is the best guess for $N$?
problems.hint: Start from the sample maximum — it underestimates $N$. Scale up by the expected gap between order statistics.
problems.solution:
The sample maximum $m = 120$ from $k = 6$ draws underestimates $N$. The $k$ observed serials split $\{1,\dots,N\}$ into gaps of expected size $\frac{N-k}{k+1}$ each, so the expected maximum is $m \approx \frac{k}{k+1}(N+1)$. Inverting gives the UMVU estimator
$$\hat N = m\left(1 + \frac1k\right) - 1 = 120 \cdot \frac76 - 1 = 139.$$
The actual WWII use of this estimator famously beat intelligence estimates of tank production.

problems.title: Russian Roulette, No Re-Spin
problems.question: A six-chambered revolver is loaded with one bullet and the cylinder is spun once to randomize the chamber order. You and your friend alternate pulling the trigger — your friend goes first — and the cylinder is never re-spun. The person who fires the bullet loses. What is the probability that you win?
problems.hint: With no re-spin, the game is equivalent to the bullet sitting in one of six fixed positions.
problems.solution:
With a single initial spin, the bullet occupies one of the 6 chamber positions uniformly. Your friend fires on positions 1, 3, 5 and you fire on positions 2, 4, 6 — three positions each:
$$P[\text{you win}] = \frac36 = \frac12.$$
Contrast with the re-spin variant, where each pull is a fresh $\frac16$ Bernoulli and the first player is at a disadvantage ($P[\text{second player wins}] = \frac{5}{11}$). Interviewers love asking which variant you'd rather go first in.

problems.title: Whisper Down the Lane
problems.question: $n$ people stand in a line. $P_1$ receives a binary message ("yes" or "no") and passes it to $P_2$, who passes it to $P_3$, and so on. Each person independently transmits the message they heard with probability $p$ and the opposite message with probability $1-p$, where $0 < p < 1$. Let $p_n$ be the probability that $P_n$ passes along the original message. Find $\lim_{n \to \infty} p_n$.
problems.hint: Write the one-step recursion for $p_n$ and find its fixed point.
problems.solution:
The message is correct at step $n+1$ if it was correct and transmitted faithfully, or incorrect and flipped:
$$p_{n+1} = p_n p + (1 - p_n)(1 - p).$$
The fixed point solves $p^* = p^* p + (1-p^*)(1-p)$, giving $p^* = \frac12$, and since $|2p - 1| < 1$ the recursion contracts toward it. After enough noisy relays, all information is lost — the limit is $\frac12$ regardless of $p$. (Only the degenerate $p \in \{0, 1\}$ cases preserve information.)

problems.title: Waiting for Heads-Heads
problems.question: A fair coin is flipped repeatedly until two heads appear in a row. What is the expected number of flips?
problems.hint: Set up states by current streak: no progress, one head, done.
problems.solution:
Let $E_0$ be the expected flips from scratch and $E_1$ after a fresh head:
$$E_0 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_1 + \tfrac12 E_0, \qquad E_1 = 1 + \tfrac12 \cdot 0 + \tfrac12 E_0.$$
Solving gives $E_1 = 4$ and $E_0 = 6$. In general, $HH\cdots H$ of length $k$ takes $2^{k+1} - 2$ flips in expectation — the self-overlap of the pattern is what makes it slower than a non-overlapping pattern of the same length.

problems.title: Letters to the Wrong Firms
problems.question: You wrote $X$ letters for recruiters at $X$ different firms, then forgot which recruiter belongs to which firm and assigned the letters to firms uniformly at random, one letter per firm. What is the expected number of letters that reach the correct firm?
problems.hint: Indicator variables — no need to touch derangement counts.
problems.solution:
Let $I_j$ indicate that letter $j$ lands correctly; $P[I_j = 1] = \frac1X$. By linearity,
$$E\left[\sum_j I_j\right] = X \cdot \frac1X = 1,$$
for every $X$. The classic matching problem: the expected number of fixed points of a random permutation is always exactly 1, even though the distribution (asymptotically Poisson(1)) is far from obvious.

problems.title: Uniform Sum Past Two
problems.question: Let $X_1, X_2, \dots \sim \mathrm{Unif}(0,1)$ IID and let $N_2 = \min\{n : X_1 + \cdots + X_n > 2\}$ be the first time the running sum exceeds 2. Find $E[N_2]$.
problems.hint: You likely know $E[N_1] = e$. Extend the renewal argument: $E[N_t]$ satisfies an integral equation on $[0, 2]$.
problems.solution:
Let $m(t) = E[N_t]$ be the expected count for threshold $t$. Conditioning on $X_1 = x$ gives the renewal equation $m(t) = 1 + \int_0^{\min(t,1)} m(t - x)\,dx$, whose solution on $[0,1]$ is the famous $m(t) = e^t$. Continuing to $t \in [1,2]$ yields
$$E[N_2] = e^2 - e \approx 4.6708.$$
(The general closed form is $m(t) = \sum_{j=0}^{\lfloor t \rfloor} (-1)^j \frac{(t-j)^j}{j!} e^{t-j}$.) The threshold-1 case is the single most famous $e$ appearance in interviews; the threshold-2 follow-up checks whether you can actually run the renewal argument.

problems.title: Fives Before the First Six
problems.question: A fair 6-sided die is rolled repeatedly until the first 6 appears. What is the expected number of 5s rolled before stopping?
problems.hint: Ignore rolls of 1–4 entirely, or condition on which of 5 and 6 appears first, and recurse.
problems.solution:
Ignore the irrelevant faces 1–4: each decisive roll is a 5 or a 6 with equal probability, and the process ends at the first 6. The number of 5s before the first 6 is then Geometric: $P[k \text{ fives}] = (\frac12)^{k+1}$, so
$$E[\#5\text{s}] = \sum_{k \ge 0} k \left(\tfrac12\right)^{k+1} = 1.$$
By the same symmetry, each of the faces 1–5 is rolled once in expectation before the first 6 — consistent with the expected $6$ total rolls.

problems.title: Waiting for H-T-H
problems.question: On average, how many tosses of a fair coin does it take to first observe the pattern HTH?
problems.hint: Compare with HH: the pattern's self-overlap (it begins and ends with H) changes the answer. States or the martingale casino both work.
problems.solution:
By Conway's leading-numbers (or the gambler-casino martingale) argument, the expected waiting time is the sum of $2^k$ over every prefix of the pattern that is also a suffix. HTH overlaps itself at length 3 (the whole word) and length 1 (single H):
$$E = 2^3 + 2^1 = 10.$$
State equations give the same answer. Compare $HTT$: only the full word overlaps, so $E = 8$ — equal-length patterns need not have equal waiting times, which is also why the Penney-ante game is non-transitive.

problems.title: The Last Bulb Standing
problems.question: Four lightbulbs have IID $\mathrm{Exp}(1)$ lifetimes $X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4$. Find the expected time until the last bulb burns out, $E[\max_i X_i]$.
problems.hint: Memorylessness: the minimum of $k$ exponentials is $\mathrm{Exp}(k)$, and after each burnout the clock restarts.
problems.solution:
The first burnout is the minimum of 4 exponentials, i.e. $\mathrm{Exp}(4)$ with mean $\frac14$. By memorylessness the remaining 3 bulbs are as good as new, so the next stage takes $\frac13$, then $\frac12$, then $1$:
$$E[\max] = \frac14 + \frac13 + \frac12 + 1 = \frac{25}{12} \approx 2.0833.$$
In general $E[\max \text{ of } n] = H_n$, the harmonic number — the continuous cousin of the coupon collector.

problems.title: Variance of the Longer Piece
problems.question: A 1-meter rope is cut at a point chosen uniformly at random along its length. Find the variance of the length of the longer piece (in meters).
problems.hint: What is the distribution of $\max(U, 1-U)$ for $U \sim \mathrm{Unif}(0,1)$?
problems.solution:
Let $U \sim \mathrm{Unif}(0,1)$ be the cut point; the longer piece is $L = \max(U, 1-U)$. By the folding symmetry, $L \sim \mathrm{Unif}(\frac12, 1)$: for $\ell \in [\frac12, 1]$, $P[L \le \ell] = P[1 - \ell \le U \le \ell] = 2\ell - 1$. A uniform on an interval of width $\frac12$ has variance
$$\mathrm{Var}(L) = \frac{(1/2)^2}{12} = \frac{1}{48} \approx 0.0208.$$
The folding trick — mapping $\max(U, 1-U)$ back to a plain uniform — kills a whole family of broken-stick questions.

problems.title: Crashes on the Half Hour
problems.question: At a busy intersection, the probability of at least one car crash during a 1-hour interval is $\frac89$. Crashes occur independently at a constant rate over time. Find the probability of at least one crash during a 30-minute interval.
problems.hint: Work with the probability of no crash — that's what multiplies across disjoint intervals.
problems.solution:
No-crash probabilities multiply across disjoint intervals: if $q$ is the no-crash probability for 30 minutes, then $q^2 = P[\text{no crash in 1 hour}] = \frac19$, so $q = \frac13$ and
$$P[\text{at least one crash}] = 1 - \frac13 = \frac23.$$
Equivalently, a Poisson process with $e^{-\lambda} = \frac19$ has $e^{-\lambda/2} = \frac13$. Never scale the event probability itself — scale the exponent.

problems.title: The Dented Die
problems.question: A fair 6-sided die has values 1–6. One side, chosen uniformly at random, has its value decreased by 1 (a 1 becomes a 0). The modified die is then rolled once. Find the probability that an even value shows.
problems.hint: Condition on which side was dented — and remember that 0 is even.
problems.solution:
Denting an odd side turns it even, and denting an even side turns it odd (with $1 \to 0$ counting as even). Denting any of 1, 3, 5 gives four even faces ($P = \frac46$); denting any of 2, 4, 6 gives two ($P = \frac26$). Averaging over the six equally likely dents:
$$P = \frac12 \cdot \frac46 + \frac12 \cdot \frac26 = \frac12.$$
The gains and losses cancel exactly — and the trap is forgetting that 0 is even.

problems.title: Pair on Top
problems.question: What is the probability that the top two cards of a well-shuffled standard 52-card deck form a pair (share the same rank)?
problems.hint: Condition on the first card — how many of the remaining 51 match it?
problems.solution:
Whatever the top card is, 3 of the remaining 51 cards match its rank:
$$P = \frac{3}{51} = \frac{1}{17} \approx 0.0588.$$
No casework needed — the first card only sets the target rank.

problems.title: Four Primes, Even Sum
problems.question: Four distinct integers are drawn uniformly at random from the first 16 primes. Find the probability that their sum is even.
problems.hint: Every prime is odd — except one.
problems.solution:
Among the first 16 primes, only 2 is even. Four odd numbers sum to an even number, while including the 2 gives even + odd + odd + odd = odd. So the sum is even exactly when 2 is not drawn:
$$P = \frac{\binom{15}{4}}{\binom{16}{4}} = \frac{12}{16} = \frac34.$$
(The ratio collapses because excluding one specific element from a 4-draw out of 16 has probability $\frac{16-4}{16}$.)

problems.title: Position in the Sundae Line
problems.question: An ice-cream shop will give a free sundae to the first person in line whose birthday matches that of someone who already bought ice cream ahead of them. Birthdays are uniform over 365 days and you know nobody else's birthday. Which position in line should you choose to maximize the probability that you win the sundae?
problems.hint: You win at position $k$ if the first $k-1$ birthdays are all distinct and yours matches one of them. Compare consecutive positions.
problems.solution:
At position $k$ you win if the $k-1$ people ahead have all-distinct birthdays and yours matches one of them:
$$P_k = \frac{365 \cdot 364 \cdots (365-k+2)}{365^{k-1}} \cdot \frac{k-1}{365}.$$
The ratio $P_{k+1}/P_k = \frac{k}{k-1}\cdot\frac{365-k+1}{365}$ exceeds 1 while $k(366-k) > 365(k-1)$, i.e. up to $k = 19$, and drops below 1 after. So the maximum is at
$$k = 20, \qquad P_{20} \approx 3.23\%.$$
The birthday-paradox threshold $\sqrt{365} \approx 19$ shows up exactly where it should.

problems.title: Penalty Kicks and Pólya's Urn
problems.question: You take 100 penalty kicks. You made kick 1 and missed kick 2. From kick 3 on, the probability you score equals the fraction of kicks scored so far (if you made 17 of the first 30, you make kick 31 with probability $\frac{17}{30}$). What is the probability that you score exactly 66 of the 100 kicks?
problems.hint: This is a Pólya urn. Work out the distribution of the final tally for a small case — say 4 kicks — and guess the pattern.
problems.solution:
This process is Pólya's urn started with one success and one failure. By exchangeability, every sequence with $m$ makes in the 98 remaining kicks has the same probability, and the total number of makes after $n$ kicks is uniform on $\{1, 2, \dots, n-1\}$. With $n = 100$:
$$P[\text{exactly } 66] = \frac{1}{99} \approx 0.0101$$
— the same for 66 as for any other count from 1 to 99. The "rich get richer" dynamics exactly cancel into a uniform final distribution.

problems.title: Beat the Dealer's Card
problems.question: You and the dealer are each dealt one card from a shuffled standard 52-card deck. You win only if your card's rank is strictly higher than the dealer's; ties and lower ranks lose. What is the probability you win?
problems.hint: By symmetry you and the dealer are equally likely to hold the higher card — so first find the probability of a tie.
problems.solution:
The two cards tie in rank with probability $\frac{3}{51}$. Otherwise, by symmetry, each player is equally likely to hold the strictly higher card:
$$P[\text{win}] = \frac{1 - 3/51}{2} = \frac{24}{51} = \frac{8}{17} \approx 0.4706.$$
The house edge lives entirely in the ties — a recurring casino-game motif.

problems.title: All Lines Odd
problems.question: The integers 1–9 are placed uniformly at random on a $3 \times 3$ grid. What is the probability that every row, every column, and both diagonals sum to an odd number?
problems.hint: Only the parity pattern matters: where can the four even numbers go so that all 8 lines contain an odd count of odd numbers?
problems.solution:
Only parities matter: there are five odd digits and four even. A line sums odd iff it contains 1 or 3 odd entries. Checking the possible placements of the four even numbers, the only pattern satisfying all 8 lines is evens on the four corners: each edge row/column then reads even–odd–even (one odd) and the middle row, middle column, and both diagonals pass through odd cells correctly. The four even positions are chosen uniformly among $\binom94 = 126$ parity patterns, exactly one of which works:
$$P = \frac{1}{126} \approx 0.0079.$$
Verified by brute force over all $9!$ placements ($2880/362880$).

problems.title: One Bulb Against Ten
problems.question: Ten independent light bulbs have lifetimes $T_i \sim \mathrm{Exp}(1/10)$ (mean 10), and an eleventh bulb has lifetime $X \sim \mathrm{Exp}(1/90)$ (mean 90). Find $P[X > T_1 + \cdots + T_{10}]$.
problems.hint: Use memorylessness one bulb at a time — or recognize $P[X > S] = E[e^{-S/90}]$ as an MGF evaluation.
problems.solution:
Condition on the sum $S = T_1 + \cdots + T_{10}$: since $X$ is exponential, $P[X > S] = E[e^{-S/90}]$, the MGF of $S$ at $-\frac{1}{90}$. Each factor contributes $\frac{1/10}{1/10 + 1/90} = \frac{9}{10}$, so
$$P = \left(\frac{9}{10}\right)^{10} \approx 0.3487.$$
Intuition via memorylessness: at each burnout the race restarts, and the long-lived bulb outlasts each $T_i$ independently with probability $\frac{90}{90+10}$.

problems.title: A Small Triangle Somewhere
problems.question: A point $P$ is chosen uniformly at random inside triangle $ABC$. Find the probability that at least one of the triangles $ABP$, $BCP$, $CAP$ has area at most one quarter of the area of $ABC$.
problems.hint: The three sub-triangle areas are the barycentric coordinates of $P$ — they're nonnegative and sum to 1.
problems.solution:
The areas of $ABP$, $BCP$, $CAP$ divided by the area of $ABC$ are exactly the barycentric coordinates $(u, v, w)$ of $P$: nonnegative, summing to 1, and uniform over the simplex. The complement event — all three fractions exceed $\frac14$ — is the sub-simplex $u, v, w > \frac14$, a triangle similar to the original with scale $1 - 3 \cdot \frac14 = \frac14$ and hence relative area $\frac{1}{16}$:
$$P = 1 - \frac{1}{16} = \frac{15}{16}.$$
Barycentric coordinates turn area questions into simplex geometry.

problems.title: Seven Bills from Eight
problems.question: A box holds one $\$100$ bill, three $\$20$ bills, and four $\$10$ bills. You draw 7 of the 8 bills uniformly at random without replacement. Find the expected value of the total you draw.
problems.hint: Linearity doesn't care about the dependence between draws.
problems.solution:
Each of the 8 bills is included with probability $\frac78$, so by linearity
$$E[\text{total}] = \frac78 (100 + 3 \cdot 20 + 4 \cdot 10) = \frac78 \cdot 200 = 175.$$
Equivalently: the one excluded bill has expected value $\frac{200}{8} = 25$. Sampling without replacement changes nothing about expectations — only about variances.

problems.title: A Call Option on Two Dice
problems.question: Two fair 6-sided dice are rolled and the underlying settles at the product of the two upfaces. What is the fair price of a call option with strike 30 on this product — that is, $E[\max(XY - 30,\, 0)]$?
problems.hint: Very few of the 36 outcomes finish in the money.
problems.solution:
Only products above 30 pay out. The candidates: $6 \times 6 = 36$ pays $6$; $5 \times 6 = 6 \times 5 = 30$ is exactly at strike and pays 0. So
$$E[\max(XY - 30, 0)] = \frac{1}{36} \cdot 6 = \frac16 \approx 0.1667.$$
Options on dice are the cleanest way interviews test whether you can price a payoff by direct enumeration — note how the strike sitting exactly on an outcome (30) is there to catch you.

problems.title: Half the Sum, Exactly
problems.question: Let $X_1, \dots, X_{40}$ be IID positive random variables with $E[X_1] = 2$ and $E\left[\frac{1}{X_1}\right] = 1$. Define $S_n = X_1 + \cdots + X_n$. Compute $E\left[\frac{S_{20}}{S_{40}}\right]$.
problems.hint: Neither given moment is needed. What is $E\left[\frac{X_i}{S_{40}}\right]$, summed over all $i$?
problems.solution:
By symmetry, $E\left[\frac{X_i}{S_{40}}\right]$ is the same for every $i$, and the 40 terms sum to $E\left[\frac{S_{40}}{S_{40}}\right] = 1$. So each equals $\frac{1}{40}$ and
$$E\left[\frac{S_{20}}{S_{40}}\right] = \frac{20}{40} = \frac12.$$
The stated values of $E[X_1]$ and $E[1/X_1]$ are pure red herrings — and note $E[S_{20}/S_{40}] \ne E[S_{20}]/E[S_{40}]$ in general; it's exchangeability, not that fallacy, doing the work.

problems.title: One Extra Roll for Primes
problems.question: Player 1 rolls a fair 6-sided die 10{,}000 times; player 2 rolls one 10{,}001 times. What is the probability that player 2 rolls strictly more primes than player 1?
problems.hint: Which faces of a die are prime — and what special probability does that give each roll?
problems.solution:
The prime faces are 2, 3, 5 — exactly half of the six — so each roll is a fair coin flip for "prime." This is the classic $n+1$ vs. $n$ fair-coin problem: after 10{,}000 flips each, ties are broken by player 2's extra flip, and by symmetry
$$P[\text{player 2 ahead}] = \frac12.$$
Formally: let $D$ be the difference after equal rolls; $P[D>0] = P[D<0]$, and the extra flip converts exactly half of the tie mass into wins. The disguise (primes on a die) is the entire problem.

problems.title: Visible Heads in a Line
problems.question: Twenty people with IID heights from a continuous distribution stand in a single-file line. Looking from the front, a person's head is visible if they are taller than everyone ahead of them. Find the expected number of visible heads.
problems.hint: When is person $k$'s head visible? An indicator and a symmetry argument each position.
problems.solution:
Person $k$ is visible iff they are the tallest of the first $k$ people, which by symmetry has probability $\frac1k$. By linearity,
$$E[X] = \sum_{k=1}^{20} \frac1k = H_{20} \approx 3.60.$$
This is the theory of records: visible heads, best-so-far stock prices, and left-to-right maxima of a permutation are all the same $H_n$.

problems.title: Long Cycles in a Random Permutation
problems.question: Let $C_n$ count the cycles of length greater than $n$ in a uniformly random permutation of $\{1, 2, \dots, 2n\}$. Compute $\lim_{n \to \infty} E[C_n]$.
problems.hint: For $k > n$ there can be at most one $k$-cycle. What is the probability a random permutation of $2n$ elements contains a cycle of exact length $k$?
problems.solution:
For $k > \frac{2n}{2} = n$, a permutation can contain at most one $k$-cycle, and the probability that a uniform permutation of $2n$ elements has a cycle of exact length $k$ is $\frac1k$ (choose the cycle: $\binom{2n}{k}\frac{k!}{k}(2n-k)!$ out of $(2n)!$). So
$$E[C_n] = \sum_{k=n+1}^{2n} \frac1k \longrightarrow \ln 2 \approx 0.6931.$$
The same harmonic tail is the engine of the 100-prisoners problem, where $1 - \ln 2 \approx 0.31$ is the survival probability.

problems.title: Local Maxima in a Random Line-Up
problems.question: Fourteen papers labelled 1–14 are arranged in a line uniformly at random. Position $i$ is a local maximum if its paper is strictly larger than the papers adjacent to it (positions at the ends have only one neighbor). Find the expected number of local maxima.
problems.hint: Ends need to beat 1 neighbor, interior spots must beat 2. Linearity over the 14 positions.
problems.solution:
An end position beats its single neighbor with probability $\frac12$; an interior position is the largest of the 3 values around it with probability $\frac13$. With 2 ends and 12 interior positions:
$$E = 2 \cdot \frac12 + 12 \cdot \frac13 = 1 + 4 = 5.$$
In general $\frac{n+1}{3}$ for $n \ge 2$ — worth deriving once and remembering the $\frac13$ heuristic for peaks.

problems.title: Redraws at Two Cents
problems.question: You draw a uniform random number from $(0,1)$. You may pay $\$0.02$ per draw to generate additional numbers, as many times as you like, seeing each result before deciding. Your payout is the maximum of all numbers drawn, minus the total fees. Under the optimal strategy, what is your expected net payout?
problems.hint: Find the threshold $t$ where one more draw's expected improvement, $\int_t^1 (x-t)\,dx$, exactly equals the fee.
problems.solution:
Stop when the current maximum $m$ exceeds a threshold $t$. One more draw improves the maximum by $\int_t^1 (x - t)\,dx = \frac{(1-t)^2}{2}$ in expectation; setting this equal to the fee $0.02$ gives $(1-t)^2 = 0.04$, so $t = 0.8$. Under this policy the number of draws is Geometric($0.2$) with mean 5 (4 of them paid), and the stopping value is $E[X \mid X \ge 0.8] = 0.9$:
$$E[\text{net}] = 0.9 - 4 \cdot 0.02 = 0.82.$$
A sweep of nearby thresholds confirms $0.8$ is optimal. The one-stage-lookahead rule is exactly optimal here because the problem is monotone.

problems.title: When the Sequence Stops Falling
problems.question: Let $X_1, X_2, \dots$ be IID with a continuous distribution, and let $N$ be the first index $n$ such that $X_1 \ge X_2 \ge \cdots \ge X_{n-1}$ but $X_{n-1} < X_n$ — the time the sequence first stops decreasing. Find $E[N]$.
problems.hint: Compute $P[N > n]$ — it only depends on the relative order of the first $n$ values.
problems.solution:
$N > n$ exactly when the first $n$ values are in decreasing order, which by symmetry has probability $\frac{1}{n!}$. Summing the tail:
$$E[N] = \sum_{n \ge 0} P[N > n] = \sum_{n \ge 0} \frac{1}{n!} = e \approx 2.7183.$$
Together with the uniform-sum problem ($E[N_1] = e$), this is the other canonical way $e$ falls out of an interview question — both are tail sums with factorial decay, and the answer doesn't depend on the distribution at all.

problems.title: Fair Price per Door
problems.question: On a game show, $\$200$ sits behind each of 2 of 7 doors; the other 5 hide nothing. You open distinct doors uniformly at random until you find the first prize. If each door opening costs $\$x$ and the game is fair, what is $x$?
problems.hint: The 2 prizes split the 5 empty doors into 3 gaps of equal expected size.
problems.solution:
By symmetry, the 2 prize doors split the 5 empty doors into 3 gaps of equal expected size $\frac53$. You open the empty doors of the first gap and then the first prize door, so
$$E[\text{doors}] = \frac53 + 1 = \frac83$$
(the general rule: the first of $k$ specials among $n$ items sits at position $\frac{n+1}{k+1}$ on average). You always win exactly one $\$200$ prize since you stop at the first one, so fairness requires $x \cdot \frac83 = 200$:
$$x = 75.$$

problems.title: Three Geometrics in Strict Order
problems.question: Ty, Guy, and Psy each flip a fair coin repeatedly until their own first heads. Let $T$, $G$, $P$ be the numbers of flips they respectively need. Find $P[T < G < P]$.
problems.hint: Sum the geometric series from the inside out — ties are what make this smaller than $\frac16$.
problems.solution:
Each variable is Geometric: $P[T = k] = 2^{-k}$. Summing over $t < g < p$ from the inside out: $\sum_{p > g} 2^{-p} = 2^{-g}$, then $\sum_{g > t} 4^{-g} = \frac13 \cdot 4^{-t}$, then
$$P[T < G < P] = \frac13 \sum_{t \ge 1} 8^{-t} = \frac13 \cdot \frac17 = \frac{1}{21}.$$
For continuous variables symmetry would give $\frac16 = \frac{3.5}{21}$; the gap is the probability mass lost to ties, which strict inequalities exclude.

problems.title: Laplace Transform of an Exit Time
problems.question: Let $W_t$ be a standard Brownian motion and let $T_a = \inf\{t > 0 : |W_t| > a\}$ be the first exit time from $(-a, a)$. The Laplace transform is $E[e^{-\lambda T_a}] = \mathrm{sech}(a\sqrt{2\lambda})$. Evaluate it at $\lambda = 4$ and $a = \ln 2$.
problems.hint: $e^{\theta W_t - \theta^2 t/2}$ is a martingale; average the two exit points and apply optional stopping.
problems.solution:
For $\theta = \sqrt{2\lambda}$, the process $M_t = \cosh(\theta W_t)\, e^{-\lambda t}$ is a martingale (average of the exponential martingales for $\pm\theta$). Optional stopping at $T_a$, where $|W_{T_a}| = a$:
$$1 = E[M_{T_a}] = \cosh(a\theta)\, E[e^{-\lambda T_a}] \implies E[e^{-\lambda T_a}] = \frac{1}{\cosh(a\sqrt{2\lambda})}.$$
At $\lambda = 4$, $a = \ln 2$: $a\sqrt{2\lambda} = 2\sqrt2 \ln 2 \approx 1.9605$, giving
$$E[e^{-4T_a}] = \mathrm{sech}(2\sqrt2 \ln 2) \approx 0.2761.$$
The two-sided barrier gives $\mathrm{sech}$; a one-sided barrier would give $e^{-a\sqrt{2\lambda}}$.

problems.title: The Frog's Favorite Number
problems.question: A frog starts at 0 and repeatedly jumps forward 1 or 2 steps, each with probability $\frac12$. Let $p_k$ be the probability the frog ever lands on position $k > 0$. Find the largest value of $p_k$.
problems.hint: Condition on the first jump to get a two-term recursion, and compute the first few values before assuming monotonicity.
problems.solution:
The frog reaches $k$ from $k-1$ (with a 1-jump) or from $k-2$ (with a 2-jump), giving $p_k = \frac12 p_{k-1} + \frac12 p_{k-2}$ with $p_0 = 1$, $p_1 = \frac12$. The sequence runs $1, \frac12, \frac34, \frac58, \frac{11}{16}, \dots$, oscillating toward $\frac23$ (each position is skipped with limiting probability tied to the mean jump $\frac32$). The maximum over $k > 0$ is
$$p_2 = \frac34.$$
The trap is answering the limit $\frac23$ — the early terms overshoot it.

problems.title: Halving Coins to a Perfect Finish
problems.question: 25 fair coins are flipped simultaneously. All coins showing tails are removed, and the survivors are flipped again — repeating until either no coins remain or all remaining coins show heads (with at least one coin present), which counts as a win. What is the probability you win?
problems.hint: Compute the win probability for 1 coin, then 2. A pattern this clean demands induction.
problems.solution:
Let $f(n)$ be the win probability with $n$ coins. Conditioning on the number of heads $k$ in a round:
$$f(n) = \frac{1}{2^n}\left(1 + \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \binom{n}{k} f(k)\right).$$
Claim: $f(n) = \frac12$ for all $n \ge 1$. It holds for $f(1) = \frac12$, and inductively
$$f(n) = \frac{1 + \frac12(2^n - 2)}{2^n} = \frac{2^n}{2^n \cdot 2} = \frac12.$$
So for 25 coins — or any number — the answer is exactly $\frac12$. The $n$-independence is the interview payoff: a sprawling-looking process with a coin-flip answer.

problems.title: Race to Three of a Kind
problems.question: Anna and Brenda repeatedly toss a fair coin. Anna wins once 3 heads have appeared in total; Brenda wins once 3 tails have appeared in total (not necessarily consecutive). What is the expected number of tosses until someone wins?
problems.hint: The game ends on toss 3, 4, or 5. Enumerate how many of the 32 length-5 sequences end at each length.
problems.solution:
The game always ends by toss 5 (five tosses force three of one side). It ends at toss 3 with two all-alike sequences among $2^3$: probability $\frac14$. Of the 32 length-5 sequences, 8 decide at toss 3, 12 at toss 4 (the deciding side has exactly 2 of the first 3, then hits: $2\binom{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{16} \cdot 32 = 12$), and 12 at toss 5. So
$$E = \frac{8 \cdot 3 + 12 \cdot 4 + 12 \cdot 5}{32} = \frac{132}{32} = \frac{33}{8} = 4.125.$$
A best-of-5 series between evenly matched sides lasts 4.125 games on average — the same computation prices sports-series length.

problems.title: The Arc That Catches the Point
problems.question: Three points are chosen uniformly at random on the circumference of the unit circle, dividing it into 3 arcs. Find the expected length of the arc that contains the fixed point $(1, 0)$.
problems.hint: The three arcs average $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ — but the arc containing a fixed point is not a typical arc.
problems.solution:
A fixed point is more likely to land in a longer arc — this is size-biased sampling, the inspection paradox. Treat $(1,0)$ as a fourth uniform point: the 4 points create 4 arcs with equal expected length $\frac{2\pi}{4}$, and the arc containing $(1,0)$ is the union of the two arcs adjacent to it:
$$E[L] = 2 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{4} = \pi,$$
half the circle — versus $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ for a typical arc. The same effect makes your bus wait longer than the average headway.

problems.title: Maximum Variance on an Interval
problems.question: Let $Y$ be any random variable taking values in $[-1, 1]$. What is the maximum possible value of $\mathrm{Var}(Y)$?
problems.hint: Push all mass to the endpoints — spread is maximized at the extremes.
problems.solution:
Since $Y^2 \le 1$, $\mathrm{Var}(Y) = E[Y^2] - E[Y]^2 \le E[Y^2] \le 1$, with equality iff $Y^2 = 1$ almost surely and $E[Y] = 0$ — that is, $Y = \pm 1$ with probability $\frac12$ each:
$$\max \mathrm{Var}(Y) = 1.$$
This is Popoviciu's inequality $\mathrm{Var} \le \frac{(b-a)^2}{4}$ in its simplest case. Variance on a bounded range is maximized by the two-point distribution at the endpoints — the same fact that bounds Bernoulli variance by $\frac14$.

problems.title: Overlap of Two Random Subsets
problems.question: Subsets $A$ and $B$ are drawn independently and uniformly from the power set of $\Omega = \{1, \dots, 20\}$. Let $N = |A \cap B|$. Compute $\frac{\mathrm{Var}(N)}{E[N]}$.
problems.hint: What is the distribution of $N$? Each element joins the intersection independently.
problems.solution:
Each element lies in $A \cap B$ independently with probability $\frac12 \cdot \frac12 = \frac14$, so $N \sim \mathrm{Bin}(20, \frac14)$. Then
$$\frac{\mathrm{Var}(N)}{E[N]} = \frac{npq}{np} = q = \frac34.$$
The Fano-factor shortcut: for any binomial, $\mathrm{Var}/E = 1 - p$, no matter what $n$ is — recognizing the distribution beats computing either moment.

problems.title: Closed Groups at the Party
problems.question: At a party, each of 50 guests puts their name in a hat and draws one name uniformly at random (a random permutation of the 50 names). Guests who draw each other in a cycle form one closed group; drawing your own name makes a group of one. What is the expected number of closed groups?
problems.hint: For each $k$, what is the probability that a fixed guest belongs to a cycle of length $k$ — and how much does one cycle of length $k$ contribute to the group count?
problems.solution:
Groups are exactly the cycles of a uniform random permutation of 50 elements. A fixed guest belongs to a cycle of length $k$ with probability $\frac{1}{50}$ for every $k$ (following the chain: $\frac{49}{50}\cdot\frac{48}{49}\cdots$ telescopes). Each guest in a $k$-cycle contributes $\frac1k$ of that group, so
$$E[\#\text{groups}] = 50 \sum_{k=1}^{50} \frac{1}{50} \cdot \frac{1}{k} = H_{50} \approx 4.50.$$
Expected cycle count of a random permutation is $H_n$ — the third face of the harmonic-number trio, alongside records and convoy clumps.

problems.title: Pricing a Straddle at Zero
problems.question: An underlying settles at $S \sim N(0, 1)$. A straddle at strike $K = 0$ pays $|S - K| = |S|$. What is the fair value of this straddle?
problems.hint: Integrate $|s|$ against the normal density — or recall the mean of the half-normal distribution.
problems.solution:
The fair value is the expected payoff:
$$E[|S|] = 2 \int_0^\infty s \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-s^2/2}\, ds = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \approx 0.7979.$$
The mean absolute value of a standard normal, $\sqrt{2/\pi} \approx 0.8$, is a number worth carrying around: it converts a normal's standard deviation into an expected absolute move, which is exactly what a straddle prices.
